risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After completion of baseline assessments, participants were randomized to one of two treatment groups by blocked randomization with stratification on gender. The random allocation sequence was provided in sealed envelopes by the study statistician (who had no contact with participants) to a staff member not involved in data collection." Comment: The study explicitly reports blocked randomization with stratification on gender and allocation sequence generation by the statistician, which are strong context clues for a proper random allocation process. Although the exact random-number method is not stated, this is sufficient to judge the sequence generation as truly random under a permissive Low-risk threshold.

Quote: "The random allocation sequence was provided in sealed envelopes by the study statistician (who had no contact with participants) to a staff member not involved in data collection." Comment: This explicitly describes a concealment mechanism using sealed envelopes and an independent statistician with no participant contact. Although the text does not say "opaque" or "sequentially numbered," the report gives a direct concealment procedure intended to prevent foreknowledge of assignment, so this supports low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The trial was technically single-blinded given that patients were aware of which intervention they were assigned to (CRM or HE)." Comment: Participants were not blinded to assignment, and the interventions (meditation vs health education) were delivered by personnel who were not blinded to treatment status; knowledge of allocation could plausibly affect many outcomes assessed in this study, including psychosocial measures, physical activity, and blood pressure/metabolic outcomes.

Quote: "Investigators and the staff collecting the data were blinded to the treatment status of the participants." Comment: Outcome assessment blinding was explicitly reported, and the main outcomes were measured by blinded staff. The primary outcome (FMD) was obtained using ultrasound and automated edge-detection software, and secondary objective outcomes included blood pressure, anthropometrics, fasting blood biomarkers, and standard questionnaires. Because the study states that those collecting data were blinded, and the key vascular/lab outcomes are objective or instrument-based, knowledge of allocation is unlikely to have influenced outcome measurement.

Quote: "Of the randomized patients, 21 and 23 participants in the CRM and HE groups, respectively, attended the 6-month visit and 19 in each group attended the 12-month visit. There was no significant difference in drop-out rate between the two randomized groups." Comment: Attrition was moderate but balanced across groups, and the authors state there was no significant difference in dropout. The trial also used ITT with mixed-effects models: "all available data were analyzed on randomized participants (n=68)... repeated measures mixed effects models... unbiased with unbalanced and missing data, so long as the missing data are non-informative (missing at random)." This makes a bias from missing outcome data unlikely.
